title: Archaic Hominin Species Buried Only Their Women
gist: A Cell study of ancient tooth proteins resolves a decade-old puzzle: the ~300,000-year-old Homo naledi remains recovered from South Africa's Rising Star Cave, which lacked sexual dimorphism, were all female.

insights.claim: A new Cell analysis of ancient proteins from Homo naledi teeth determined all the recovered remains from Rising Star Cave were female, solving a decade-long puzzle over the species' apparent lack of sexual dimorphism.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Paleoproteomics enables sex/biological inference from fossils where DNA is unavailable; basic science with no market read.
insights.quote: just like the underground astronauts who recovered their bones, they were all female
insights.locator: Now, a new analysis of their remains published in Cell

insights.claim: Homo naledi, a ~300,000-year-old hominin from the Dinaledi Chamber of Rising Star Cave, had a curious mix of features — upright walking, human-like hands and faces, but smaller Australopithecus-like skulls.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Adds to the mosaic-evolution picture of human ancestry.
insights.quote: H. naledi walked upright and had human-like hands and faces, but also smaller skulls like Australopithecus.
insights.locator: The bones they recovered belonged to the Homo naledi
